title: ThumbJoy 3-Wire Bridge: ATtiny85 I2C Circuit Design
overview: A tiny circuit board (~8mm Г— 6mm) inside the ThumbJoy grip that reads the dual Melexis Hall sensor analog outputs, digitizes them with a 10-bit ADC, and sends the data down to the Teensy 4.1 over I2C вҖ” using only 3 wires through the HF55 hollow shaft (VCC + GND + SDA/SCL on shared wire pair).

gripSide.title: GRIP PCB (inside top handle housing)

passives.placement: As close to pin 8 as possible

gripSide.notes: Board mounts flat next to the Melexis bracket inside the grip housing. Can be secured with a dab of hot glue or double-sided tape. Keep away from Z-axis magnet zone.

shaftWiring.title: THROUGH-SHAFT WIRING (3 wires)

wires.notes: Powers ATtiny85 + Melexis sensor. ~25mA total draw.
Bidirectional I2C data. Pulled up to VCC by 4.7kО© on grip PCB.

shaftWiring.i2cNote: Standard I2C uses 2 data wires (SDA + SCL). However, at this short distance (~10-15cm shaft) and low speed (100kHz), we can use the ATtiny85's USI in TWI mode which can operate with the SCL generated by the master and only SDA running through the shaft. The Teensy 4.1 generates SCL вҖ” both sides see it. Total: 3 wires.
shaftWiring.alternative3Wire: If true 2-wire I2C (SDA+SCL) is needed, run SCL as a 4th wire OR use software UART instead: ATtiny TX вҶ’ Teensy RX = only 1 data wire. UART is simpler firmware and more noise-tolerant.
shaftWiring.recommendedProtocol: UART (Software Serial) вҖ” simplest, only needs TX pin, Teensy reads on any digital pin via SoftwareSerial or hardware Serial port. 3 wires total: VCC + GND + TX.

teensySide.title: TEENSY 4.1 (in base housing вҖ” existing hardware)
teensySide.connection: Wire 3 (SDA/TX) connects to any available I2C or Serial RX pin on the Teensy 4.1
teensySide.i2cPins: Teensy 4.1 I2C: Wire (pins 18/SDA, 19/SCL) or Wire1 (pins 17/SDA1, 16/SCL1) or Wire2 (pins 25/SDA2, 24/SCL2)
teensySide.uartPins: Teensy 4.1 Serial: Serial1 (pin 0/RX1), Serial2 (pin 7/RX2), Serial3 (pin 15/RX3), etc.
teensySide.notes: Choose whichever bus is free on the existing ThumbJoy PCB v3. If all I2C buses are in use (display, etc.), UART is cleanest.

gripFirmware.title: ATtiny85 Firmware (Grip Side)
gripFirmware.library: TinyWireS (for I2C slave) OR SoftwareSerial (for UART)
gripFirmware.flashUsage: ~1.5 KB of 8 KB (ATtiny85 has plenty of room)

teensyFirmware.title: Teensy 4.1 Firmware Changes (Base Side)
teensyFirmware.backwardsCompatible: Can add #ifdef to support both direct analog and I2C/UART modes

pcbDesign.title: Grip PCB Layout Notes
pcbDesign.tool: KiCad or Fusion ECAD (Alec uses Fusion)
layoutNotes: Decoupling caps on same side as ATtiny, as close to VCC/GND pins as possible
3 pads on one edge for shaft wires (VCC, GND, SDA) вҖ” through-hole or SMD pads
2 pads on opposite edge for Melexis analog inputs (short jumper wires to sensor)
Optional: 3 programming pads (MOSI, MISO, SCK) for ISP вҖ” only needed once
Ground pour on bottom layer for EMI shielding from Z-axis magnet
assemblyNotes: Hand-solder all components (5 SMD parts total вҖ” straightforward)
Or order JLCPCB SMT assembly (~$8 extra for 5 boards fully assembled)
Conformal coat entire board for moisture protection (spray-on works)
Secure in grip housing with Kapton tape or small dab of silicone

breadboardPrototype.title: Step 1: Breadboard Validation
parts: 1Г— Sparkfun Tiny AVR Programmer ($16) or Arduino Uno as ISP
1Г— Teensy 4.1 (or any Arduino for initial test)
steps: 1. Set up ATtiny85 on breadboard with 5V power
2. Connect 2 pots to ADC pins (PB3, PB4) вҖ” simulate Melexis 0-5V
3. Connect SDA (PB0) and SCL (PB2) to Teensy I2C bus with pull-ups
4. Flash ATtiny85 with I2C slave firmware using programmer
5. Flash Teensy with I2C master read code
6. Open Serial Monitor вҖ” verify pot values read correctly on Teensy
7. Turn pots slowly вҖ” verify smooth, glitch-free readings
8. Verify latency: toggle a pin on ATtiny when reading, measure on scope
successCriteria: Latency < 5ms from analog change to Teensy read
No I2C communication errors over 10 minutes
Smooth tracking with no glitches or dropped packets

integrationTest.title: Step 2: Integration with Real ThumbJoy
steps: 1. Disconnect existing 6 Melexis wires from ThumbJoy shaft
2. Dead-bug solder ATtiny85 onto small perfboard (~8Г—6mm)
3. Connect ATtiny ADC inputs to actual Melexis sensor outputs
4. Route 3 wires (VCC, GND, SDA) through HF55 shaft
5. Connect to Teensy I2C bus on ThumbJoy PCB v3
6. Update Teensy firmware to read from I2C instead of direct analog
7. Test thumbwheel response вҖ” should feel identical to direct wiring
8. Test through full Z-axis twist range вҖ” verify no EMI interference

productionPCB.title: Step 3: Custom PCB
steps: 1. Design PCB in KiCad/Fusion ECAD (8mm Г— 6mm, 2-layer)
2. Order from JLCPCB (~$2/5pcs, or $10 with SMT assembly)
3. Solder or receive assembled boards
4. Flash ATtiny85 firmware
5. Install in grip housing
6. Conformal coat for moisture protection
7. Final integration test

emInterference.risk: Z-axis magnet may interfere with I2C signals
emInterference.mitigation: I2C is digital and differential вҖ” much more noise-immune than analog. Ground pour on PCB bottom layer. If issues occur, switch to UART (single TX wire, even more robust).
emInterference.fallback: UART firmware is provided as alternative вҖ” trivial to swap.

powerBudget.risk: ATtiny85 + Melexis may exceed shaft wire current capacity
powerBudget.mitigation: ATtiny85 draws ~5mA at 8MHz. Melexis draws ~10-15mA. Total ~20mA. 30AWG wire handles 860mA. Massive margin.
powerBudget.fallback: N/A вҖ” not a real risk.

mechanicalFit.risk: 8mm Г— 6mm PCB may not fit next to Melexis bracket
mechanicalFit.mitigation: Board is thinner than a fingernail. Can be mounted vertically if needed. Dead-bug soldering also works for V1.
mechanicalFit.fallback: Reduce to 6mm Г— 4mm by using ATtiny412 (smaller QFN package) instead of ATtiny85 SOIC-8.

reliability.risk: Additional component in harsh marine environment
reliability.mitigation: ATtiny85 is industrial-rated (-40В°C to +85В°C). Conformal coat protects against moisture. No moving parts. MTBF is essentially infinite for this simple circuit.
reliability.fallback: Keep direct 6-wire path as backup option on PCB (add solder jumpers).
